data.title: RDVY: Outperforms Peers Despite Lower Dividend Yield
data.description:
Overview

Dividend-paying companies can provide a sense of stability through market uncertainty. High-quality dividend-paying companies that have healthy operating margins and free cash flows can continue to provide dividends whether markets are going up, down, or sideways. However, there are thousands of dividend stocks in the world, and it can be difficult to determine whether or not a company has healthy dividend metrics. First Trust Rising Dividend Achievers ETF (NASDAQ:RDVY) makes the process easy and provides a diverse range of exposure to some of the highest quality dividend-paying companies that have the potential for growth.

RDVY has an expense ratio of 0.48% and has an inception dating back to 2014, meaning there's over a decade's worth of operating history to reference. Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that RDVY has a total return of over 12%. When I previously covered RDVY, I issued a buy rating due to the ETF's resilience throughout different market cycles. The ETF has effectively recovered from the prior market decline in response to President Trump's tariff proposals throughout March and April, which reinforces the idea that RDVY's strategy is quite effective.

Although RDVY focuses on dividend-paying companies, the starting dividend yield is low at 1.6%. Therefore, the appeal of RDVY isn't in the starting income potential. The real magic of RDVY is behind the potential for strong dividend growth that can compound over time. If you are a long-term investor who is interested in a growing stream of tax-efficient dividend income, RDVY's growth potential can compete with some of the most notable peers out there. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that RDVY implements to generate its earnings and provide dividend growth to investors.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest overview, RDVY has total net assets of $15.9B that are spread across 76 different holdings. Since RDVY has less than 100 holdings, the ETF offers a more focused approach to its holdings and can translate to outperformance if the fund selects its holdings efficiently. Looking at the breakdown by sector, Financials makes up the largest portion of assets at 31.21%. This is followed by exposure to Technology and Industrial companies, accounting for 20.11% and 16.89% of assets respectively. The combination of these top three sectors makes up over 68% of RDVY, so we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on RDVY's daily price change and total return potential.

The ETF rebalances and reconstitutes its holdings every single quarter, which keeps RDVY exposed to the highest quality holdings that match the underlying criteria. For instance, RDVY starts its search for holdings by reviewing a basket of 750 stocks within the Nasdaq US Benchmark Index. The fund eliminates any real estate holdings and then filters down potential holdings to companies that have paid a dividend in the trailing twelve-month period, greater than the trailing twelve-month dividend that was paid three and five years prior. In other words, RDVY wants to maintain exposure to companies that have built a reputation for consistently growing its dividend. Some other important criteria are:

  • A company must have a cash-to-debt ratio greater than 50%
  • The trailing twelve-month dividend payout ratio cannot be more than 65%.
  • Earnings per share must be higher than three years prior.

As a result, we can see a diverse mix of holdings in many recognizable large-cap companies. eBay (EBAY) is currently the largest holding, with a weight of 2.53%. This is followed by varying weights to other large-cap companies like Meta Platforms (META), The Bank of New York Mellon (BK), Booking Holdings (BKNG), and Unum Group (UNM) just to name a few. I like the fact that RDVY doesn't put any arbitrary rules that a company must have paid out a dividend for a specific number of years in a row to be eligible. The willingness to include newer dividend companies, like META, better aligns the fund to participate in capital appreciation over time.

Even though RDVY includes exposure to some well-known growth companies, I still wouldn't expect RDVY to demonstrate rapid price growth. This unique blend between growth companies that pay a dividend with other companies that have a more established history of dividend growth but slower price growth, provides investors with the best of both worlds. Many other dividend ETFs lack meaningful exposure to technology holdings, so the price can underperform during strong bull markets, which are most often led by technology companies.

Performance Comparison

One of the most popular dividend growth ETFs is Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), which I previously published an in-depth educational piece on. SCHD's structure focuses on companies that have healthy cash flow to debt ratios, attractive five-year dividend growth rates, and a good track record of return on equity. SCHD has grown to become one of the staple ETFs of the dividend investing community for good reason. However, I believe that RDVY gives SCHD a good run for its money in some aspects.

Looking at the quick total return comparison, we can see that RDVY significantly outperforms SCHD in both price growth and total return, including dividends paid. RDVY has a total return since inception of 270.5%, compared to SCHD's total return of 216.7% over the same period. I believe a major factor in RDVY's outperformance is the exposure to technology companies.

In comparison, SCHD has a weight of 7.9% of assets that are allocated towards technology stocks. SCHD doesn't have exposure to dividend-paying companies like META or Microsoft (MSFT) because of the requirement that a company must have paid out dividends for ten consecutive years, and they must have attractive five-year dividend growth.

SCHD has a higher starting yield of 3.9%, so it may be attractive for investors looking for instant gratification from the income department. SCHD has been able to deliver dividend increases at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 11.30% over the last decade, which has earned it an A+ rating in its dividend grade. Even on a smaller time frame of five years, SCHD has been able to increase its dividend at a CAGR of 11.44%. This double-digit growth means that investors can efficiently compound their annual dividend income over time with very little effort.

However, I believe that RDVY offers a very competitive dividend growth. Despite the starting dividend yield being much lower at 1.6%, RDVY has delivered a solid dividend CAGR of 8.58% over the last ten years. More impressively, RDVY has been able to increase its dividend at a CAGR of 20.30% over the last three years! If the fund delivers stronger growth like this over the next several decades, investors may be able to see their dividend income compound significantly over time.

Although the payouts have continuously increased over time, we can see from the dividend history that all of the payouts can be slightly variable in their amount. Therefore, RDVY may not be the best choice for an investor who seeks similar and easy-to-predict payout amounts every single quarter.

Just to give an example of the income growth potential, I ran a backtest to compare the annual dividend income levels for both RDVY and SCHD if an initial investment of $25K was made at the start of 2016. On the graph below, the blue bar represents the income received from RDVY while the green bar represents the income received from SCHD.

The calculation assumes that no additional capital was ever invested after the initial $25k. However, the calculation does assume that all dividends received were reinvested back into each respective holding to accumulate more shares. The results are as follows:

2016 Dividend Income

  • RDVY: $620 in annual dividend income.
  • SCHD: $825 in annual dividend income

2024 Dividend Income

  • RDVY: $1,364, representing a total increase of 120% since 2016.
  • SCHD: $2,541, representing a total increase of 208% since 2016.

The downside here is that these ETFs rely on a large time commitment for the snowball to start rolling. Investors can expedite the process by actively adding new capital to their position on a monthly basis, which I would recommend if you are a long way off from retirement. RDVY also offers tax-efficient dividends since the income is classified as qualified. This can make RDVY a great choice for investors who do not want to deal with the tax burden that can be associated with higher-yielding income funds.

Vulnerabilities

Although RDVY can outperform other dividends-focused peers like SCHD, the ETF still has a vulnerability to underperformance against more traditional index-tracking ETFs. If you are a younger investor who has time on their side, you would likely be better off focusing on an ETF that aligns you with the highest possibility to achieve the maximum total return possible. By focusing on a more defensive dividend fund like RDVY, your portfolio may be lacking the sizable technology exposure it may need to keep up with market momentum.

Just as a point to demonstrate, I pulled in a return comparison between RDVY and more traditional index-tracking ETFs, such as SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) and Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ). As we can see below, RDVY underperforms both SPY and QQQ in return. Dividend-paying companies aren't usually known for their rapid growth abilities, since they are reinvesting a lesser amount of capital back into the growth of their business. Therefore, there aren't typically a lot of technology companies that have preferable dividend metrics, which leaves many dividend-focused ETFs underperforming the S&P 500 and Nasdaq over time.

This limited exposure to technology companies will almost certainly lead to RDVY shareholders missing out on some of the rapid growth action that is led by companies pursuing the implementation of AI. For instance, the size of the AI market is estimated to increase at a CAGR of 19.20% through 2034, according to Precedence Research. RDVY's current holdings don't include companies that do not pay dividends. Therefore, investors are missing exposure to growth companies like Amazon (AMZN), which can lead to underperformance.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on RDVY because of its strong dividend growth potential, efficient selection process, and track record of outperforming a notable peer like SCHD. However, the ETF is best utilized by investors who specifically want to focus on a growing stream of dividend income. RDVY's dividend yield is quite small at 1.6%, but the ETF has been able to provide consistent double-digit dividend growth in recent years. The dividends received from RDVY are also tax-friendly. Despite RDVY having some exposure to technology companies, the ETF is still likely to underperform traditional index-tracking ETFs like SPY or QQQ over the long term due to its emphasis on dividend-paying companies. Despite this, I believe that RDVY still has an attractive value proposition.

data.title: Kroger: Investing In Digital Channel And Fresh Category, Initiating With Buy
data.description:
The Kroger Co. (NYSE:KR) operates 2,700 grocery stores in the U.S. market, with 51% of supermarkets housed in company-owned facilities. The company just delivered a stronger Q1 FY25 result, driven by growth in pharmacy, eCommerce and fresh categories. I think Kroger is suitable for investors seeking low-volatility investments, and I am initiating with a Buy rating with a fair value of $80 per share.

As detailed in the table below, Kroger’s organic sales growth has been in the low-single-digit level in the post-pandemic period. Compared with Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST) and Walmart Inc. (WMT), Kroger’s revenue growth has lagged over the past four years, which demonstrates that Kroger has been losing market share in the US grocery market.

To accelerate its same-store sales growth, the management has increased investments in fresh and e-commerce markets. In 2024, the company boosted its operating efficiencies in its distribution centers to improve the inventory turnover rate for fresh categories. In addition, Kroger aimed to improve its sourcing capabilities through improved supply chain operations and efficiencies. I think these initiatives have started to bear fruit in FY25.

The company released its Q1 FY25 result on June 20, posting 3.2% same-store sales growth, an acceleration from previous quarters, as detailed in the table below. The management attributed the business recovery to its robust growth in fresh, e-commerce and pharma categories.

Kroger divested its Specialty Pharmacy business for $464 million in October 2024, in preparation for a merger with Albertsons Companies, Inc. (ACI), a grocery store chain. However, Albertsons called off the merger and sued Kroger in December 2024 after a federal judge blocked the deal. So basically, Kroger ended up with nothing from the potential consolidation of the grocery market. Worst of all, they divested the Specialty Pharmacy, and caused some damage to its overall pharma business unit.

Furthermore, e-commerce represents less than 9% of total revenue. The company continues to invest in its digital channel to penetrate more households with a wider range of online items. Its digital channel sales grew by 10% in FY24, which has become a major revenue and margin growth driver for Kroger. As noted in the earnings call, digital customers spend nearly 3x as much as non-digital customers. As such, the digital channel is crucial for the business acceleration. I anticipate the company will continue investing in its logistics and online SKU optimization to grow its e-commerce business.

The management raised its identical sales growth (same-store sales growth) guidance to 2.25%-3.25% for FY25, as detailed in the slide below. The raised guidance demonstrates the management’s confidence in accelerated growth in digital and fresh/other categories.

I forecast Kroger’s same-store sales will grow by 3% for FY25, reflecting a weak economic environment and sluggish consumption. From FY26 onwards, I anticipate the company’s same-store sales will grow by 4%, aligning with the average growth rate over the past six years. The growth assumes 2% grocery price growth and 2% volume growth. I anticipate a 10bps annual margin expansion driven by a higher revenue mix towards the digital channel, which carries a higher gross margin. I calculate the total operating costs will grow by 3.9% annually, leading to a 10bps margin expansion.

With these assumptions, the DCF can be summarized as follows:

I calculate FCFF as follows:

I estimate the WACC to be 8.3% assuming a risk-free rate of 4.2%; beta 1; equity risk premium of 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $43 bn; debt $18bn; tax rate of 23%. I set the terminal growth rate to be 4%, assuming 2% pricing growth and 2% volume growth. Discounting all the future FCFF and adjusting net debt, the fair value is calculated to be $80 per share, as per my estimates.

Risks

As discussed previously, Kroger’s growth is significantly lower than some competitors like Costco and Walmart. Kroger’s management needs to effectively implement their plans for the digital channel and continue investing in growth categories such as fresh and pharma. Without growth acceleration, the market is unlikely to assign a higher multiple to Kroger’s stock price. It’s worth noting that Kroger is trading at a much lower multiple compared to the sector median level, as shown below:

In addition, due to the weak consumption, Kroger’s business is more likely to face challenges in discretionary categories. As noted during the earnings call, the management anticipates a continuing slowdown in consumer spendings on discretionary items.

As a grocery retailer, Kroger will face higher costs from tariff duties. However, I think the company could mitigate the risk via price increases and adjustments in its supply chain. During the call, the management noted that their tariff impact will be much smaller than its peers as Kroger only has a single-digit percentage of sourcing from China.

Conclusion

I think The Kroger Co.’s management is on the right track to streamline its operations and focus on e-commerce and key growth areas. I am initiating KR with a Buy rating with a fair value of $80 per share.

data.title: Salesforce: Resilient Q1 Results Overshadowed By A Poor Acquisition
data.description:
Thesis:

Salesforce’s (NYSE: CRM) acquisition of Informatica (NYSE: INFA) and Q1 FY2026 results have resulted in significant investor pullback, driven largely by pessimism towards AI solutions. While I share some of the concerns surrounding the acquisition, I believe the market’s pessimism toward Salesforce is largely overstated. I maintain a 'Hold' view on CRM - the company remains fundamentally strong and highly profitable, with strong growth and momentum in its Data Cloud and AI offerings.

However, its renewed push into M&A raises concerns about long-term strategic focus. There are underlying integration issues in their existing line of products, which do not form a coherent ecosystem, while historic acquisitions have not yet yielded the expected returns. I do not think an aggressive M&A expansion helps address these fundamental issues, and would prefer a more organic growth route from Salesforce.

Salesforce’s Q1 Results:

Salesforce’s non-GAAP EPS grew 5.8% YoY to $2.58 and revenue showed a convincing 8% YoY growth to $9.8bn - both metrics slightly beating analyst expectations. So why the investor pullback?

The concerns are based on fears over AI industry headwinds, which Salesforce appears to be facing as evidenced by its guidance to Q2 FY2026. The highly concerning statistic is the forecasted cRPO YoY growth of 9% — a decline compared to the 12% YoY growth in Q1. This small decrease has raised questions regarding the effectiveness of AI tools, such as EinsteinCopilot, in driving Salesforce’s growth – if they are so transformative then why is cRPO growth falling?

The investor sentiment is reinforced by underwhelming performance of specific AI tools. While Data Cloud and AI reached over $1bn ARR (a 120% YoY growth) and Agentforce reached $100m ARR - clearly showing very rapid expansion, this is almost insignificant compared to the company's total revenue of $37.9bn in FY2025. Investors clearly expect AI software to show more promising near-term results while retaining long-term upside, which it is failing to do.

The raised guidance for FY2026 does not address the structural problem Salesforce is facing. Historically it has presented AI solutions to be some of its new long-term growth drivers, so their success is critical to its ability to maintain premium valuation and investor confidence.

The skepticism is reinforced by Salesforce’s mediocre capital efficiency. I estimate its ROIC to have been 11.1% in FY2025, which exceeds its WACC of 8.32% but is not as impressive as some of its comparable companies. For example, AppLovin, a software company focused on mobile app monetization and advertising (not a direct competitor but one of Salesforce's peers), achieved a 48.6% ROIC in FY2024 (roughly the same time period). An LTM asset turnover ratio of 0.4x, compared to the industry median of 0.6x, and ROA of 6.29% further strengthen this consensus.

I do not entirely agree with the investor sentiment here. While it is undeniable that AI software hasn’t become a key driver of Salesforce’s expansion just yet, I believe its growth is highly underappreciated. This is the second consecutive quarter in which Data Cloud and AI have achieved 120% YoY ARR growth, having reached $900m in Q4 FY2025 – signifying that their expansion is not only rapid but continuous as well. Agentforce alone is speculated to drive cRPO growth by 1-2% in the second half of FY2026. This sentiment is reinforced by historical improvement in capital efficiency, with ROA showing 88.20% improvement over the 5-year average and estimated LTM ROIC increasing from 11.1% in Q4 FY2025 to 12.2% in Q1 FY2026.

Admittedly, the asset turnover ratio only showed marginal improvement from its 5Y average of 0.36x.

I believe the investor pullback is more reflective of growing macroeconomic uncertainty and pessimism towards AI software than it is of structural problems within Salesforce. I maintain a positive outlook on the transformational capabilities of Salesforce's AI and Data cloud tools, which have shown very high, sustainable growth and will likely account for multi-billion ARR in FY2027. Their growth is only undermined by falling growth of cRPO’s, but I believe the former would be more likely caused by market uncertainty than by intrinsic weakness in Salesforce’s product line or execution. The company remains incredibly profitable while its capital efficiency is showing substantial improvement, suggesting it has not yet entered the final stages of its growth cycle. I think the significance of AI software to Salesforce’s offerings will be more obvious in Q3 FY2026, when Agentforce would have had more time to mature and become a key driver of Salesforce’s ARR growth. Until then, I do not find the current market pessimism toward Salesforce’s AI initiatives to be conclusive.

The other cause for investors’ concern is Salesforce’s announced acquisition of Informatica, which I will to analyze in more detail.

Informatica’s Market Position

Informatica (INFA) is one of the more mature players in the data infrastructure space — a fact reflected in both its valuation and growth profile. The company delivered very modest revenue growth of 4.2%, 6% and 2.8% in fiscal years 2022, 2023 and 2024 respectfully. This growth has been primarily driven by a shift toward a subscription-based business model, partially offset by the continued decline in legacy maintenance revenue. Its strong normalized EBITDA margins (33.6% in FY2024) further reflect its position as an operationally efficient company.

Informatica’s valuation is not driven by the forward outlook on its potential, as it is already approaching the later stages of the growth cycle, but is instead derived by the stability of its core operations. Its cloud net retention rate of 120% and maintenance renewal rate of 93% for the 3 months ended March 31st 2025 demonstrate strong customer loyalty and consistent, recurring revenue streams. At the same time, no customer represented over 10% of revenue in Q1 FY2025, signifying a highly diversified portfolio.

Informatica’s Trajectory

While Informatica’s overall revenue growth has been slowing, its cloud subscription ARR has shown an impressive 30.1% YoY growth. The reason for this is Informatica’s strong alignment with global structural changes. Global spending on cloud-based application software is showing 20.12% CAGR, as opposed to 3.65% CAGR of spending on on-premises application software, clearly signifying a structural shift away from traditional on-premises software. The shift is driven by cloud-based solutions’ larger scalability and smaller upfront costs.

The benefit derived from this trend is enhanced by the increasing global adoption of AI-driven workflows. Global spending on cloud AI software is showing a CAGR of 20.4%, with one of the key drivers being its applications in enterprise-wide ERP solutions. These two trends reflect broader changes in the enterprise software industry.

Informatica positions itself to strongly benefit from these shifts in the long-term. While they may have caused short-term growth decline, given the falling revenues from maintenance services, they greatly elevate Informatica’s prolonged potential and demonstrate its adaptability to structural changes. This is complemented nicely by the nature of the subscription business model, which offers visibility into ARR, and leads to more predictable cash flows.

Strategic Fit to Salesforce

Salesforce positions itself strongly with structural implementation of AI software, exemplified by one of its core products - EinsteinCopilot. However, it lacks the native control over how data is managed and integrated across multi-cloud environments.

That’s exactly where Informatica comes in, whose Intelligent Data Management Cloud (IDMC) is designed to bring data from hybrid environments. Informatica’s acquisition is not aimed to add another product to Salesforce’s portfolio, but to instead enhance all of its existing offerings. The quality of the delivered data is very important to Salesforce’s AI tools, which cannot otherwise offer valuable insights, and appear to be the main focus behind the acquisition.

The acquisition becomes more compelling given the suitability of Informatica’s business model to Salesforce’s ecosystem. It’s cloud-native, resulting in more flexibility and scalability, and integrates with platforms like AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud - just like Salesforce does. Both companies also primarily operate under a SaaS subscription-based revenue model, so that is a natural fit as well.

Pessimism Towards the Acquisition

The acquisition was announced on May 27th with a price of $25 per share, which represents a 34.6% premium over the 30-day moving average on May 22nd and 30.20% over the closing price of the same day (before rumors of the acquisition inflated the price).

While strategic in nature, the deal imposes considerable pressure on Salesforce’s balance sheet. I estimate that cash and cash equivalents will decline from $17.4 billion to $12.9 billion, while long-term debt will rise from $8.4 billion to $12.7 billion, (assuming 30% of the transaction is debt-financed and 70% is from cash reserves). That said, with cost of revenues at just $2.27 billion in Q1 FY2026, Salesforce retains ample liquidity to meet its near-term obligations.

Informatica’s ~$600 million in operating profit translates to only a ~7% return on the $8 billion investment, which has contributed to investor concerns about valuation and capital efficiency.

However, the majority of the pessimism is derived from the strategic thinking behind the acquisition. Salesforce has historically shown a preference for inorganic growth, with other notable acquisitions being those of Tableau (SaaS data visualization platform, $15.9 billion), MuleSoft (application integration software company, $6.5 billion), and Slack (business messaging and collaboration platform, $27.7 billion) - incorporating their offerings into its platform. While it has somewhat shifted from this strategy after the Slack acquisition in 2021, it seems to be making a return now. The inorganic growth strategy has been widely criticized for diverging Salesforce away from its roots of CRM and sales automation to enterprise software, while struggling to produce a unified ecosystem for its solutions. Even the previously mentioned acquired platforms seem to operate as individual modules, as opposed to a unified product.

Another concern is the opportunity cost behind the acquisition. The $8 billion cash could have been spent on improving Salesforce’s existing products, which also includes those from their past acquisitions. At its core, Informatica’s purchase deeply favors Salesforce’s AI solutions, but the logical question of whether this was the best way of improving these offerings rises almost immediately. Could internal R&D not have achieved the same or greater improvement of AI products on the same $8 billion budget? While the strategic benefit is present, the possibility of this being a hasty purchase made during the AI trend is not as low as I would like it to be.

It should be emphasized that at this point Data Cloud and AI only contribute $1 billion ARR to Salesforce’s revenues while Informatica’s acquisition, which only complements one aspect of AI software’s productivity, cost the company $8 billion. While the deal has been made with a more forward outlook, its diminishing short-term impacts are undeniable.

Risks

There is a significant integration risk between the companies. First of all, Informatica slightly overlaps in functionality with MuleSoft, which was acquired by Salesforce in 2018. Although MuleSoft primarily focuses on application integration, as opposed to data integration like Informatica, there will be inherent overlap which could result in partial cannibalization of either platform if integration is mishandled. There could be customer confusion, internal competition etc. which would hurt both businesses. Secondly, Informatica has many customers who may value its neutrality (SAP, Oracle etc.). Integrating it as a ‘Salesforce-only’ solution would result in customer pullback and significantly hurt its valuation. I believe Salesforce needs to maintain Informatica almost as an independent platform, with assurances to existing clients that the acquisition will not affect the service they pay for, with an additional integration into Salesforce’s existing platform. While Salesforce has experience with these kinds of mergers, their track record remains mixed. As such, their ability to integrate Informatica into their ecosystem remains a big challenge to their ability to deliver long-term value.

More obviously, there is a risk of AI software failing to show substantial growth. Agentforce was released in September 2024 so is expected to drive more substantial growth in the second half of FY2026. If it fails to do so, while other tools also see a decline in their respective cRPO’s, it will result in disproportionally large investor pullback while also harming Salesforce’s long-term potential.

CRM Valuation

My DCF analysis suggests a share price of $327,5, assuming a WACC of 8.3% and terminal growth rate of 3%. The sensitivity analysis, shown in the table above, suggests prices of $294.8, $328.8 and $376.7 for the 25th, 50th and 75th percentiles of the data respectfully.

My assumptions included accelerated revenue growth of 10.3% in FY2027, reflecting my expectation for AI software driven expansion, but slower growth in FY2026 given macroeconomic headwinds. I think the AI software market will likely be a revenue driver until early 2030's, peaking in FY2027, but will likely converge to Salesforce's terminal growth rate soon after that.

I do not think Salesforce will experience further significant margin expansion, given it has already achieved a high normalised operating profit margin of 33.0% in FY2025.

As such, I maintain a 'Hold' rating on Salesforce with a target share price of around $325.

Conclusion

While I am critical of the Informatica acquisition, I remain confident in Salesforce’s overall trajectory. Its growth so far has been largely positive, and I do not share the market pessimism towards AI.

What I want to see more of is consistent, and largely organic growth. Salesforce already has an array of tools with long-term potential – many of which come from its past acquisitions, and I think taking more time to integrate them into one ecosystem while maintaining their competitiveness through internal R&D would be more beneficial than pursuing product line expansion through aggressive M&A. I am less concerned about Informatica’s strategic fit to Salesforce, which I believe is largely present if AI software continues to scale, and more about Salesforce’s overall expansion strategy.

I am reasonably confident in Salesforce’s ability to deliver results of AI software that investors have to expect in mid to late FY2026, although I think this would be a big challenge. If it manages to do so, and consequently retains investor confidence, AI software will likely become a big driver of its growth as soon as FY2027. As such, I retain an optimistic outlook on Salesforce’s future.

data.title: Renault Leadership Vacuum, A Short-Term Headwind That Could Prove Challenging Long Term
data.description:
My, how Renault SA’s (OTCPK:RNSDF) prospects have taken a turn for the worse over the past quarter-century. In 1999, when the French automaker rode courageously to the rescue of debt laden Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY), the two automakers – joined by a mutual share exchange, though short of a full-fledged merger – looked very much like an enterprise with the breadth, mass, creativity and energy to challenge the industry’s top tier.

Sadly, together they were unable to fulfill the alliance’s potential, and now both are struggling. With production of about 1.5 million light commercial vehicles in 2024 Renault is barely generating enough cash and income on its own to cover the massive R&D outlays needed to modernize existing models while complying with regulations governing electrification and the transition to zero-emission technology.

The French lost Peugeot to the newly formed Stellantis in 2020, so Renault – which owns the Alpine and Dacia brands – remains the last independent, mass market French automaker standing. For how much longer?

Renault’s annual revenue last year of $52.4 billion (down slightly from 2023) has fallen well below the revenue levels of 2017, 2018 and 2019. Net income is down from 2017 and 2018 levels, as are R&D outlays.

Leadership, A Revolving Door

The 2018 indictment that engulfed onetime CEO Carlos Ghosn in many ways reflected the tension between the two companies over alleged financial misconduct, misuse of company assets and breach of trust. The two automakers, plus Mitsubishi (which had joined the alliance in 2016), never were realizing synergies and relative strengths to their full potential. Ghosn’s abrupt departure only intensified doubts on all sides, stoking C-suite rivalries.

DeMeo, a career veteran whose last stop was Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY), was hired in 2020 to lead Renault; he managed to restore solid profit in 2021, 2023 and 2024 by largely revamping the automaker’s product lineup, per Automotive News:

De Meo is leaving Renault at the peak of a new model cycle. In addition to the Renault 5, launched last autumn, key new Renault Group cars include the Renault 4 small electric SUV, Scenic electric compact SUV, revamped Dacia Duster and new Bigster and Alpine A390, a midsize electric crossover that is the group’s flagship model and a rival to the Porsche Macan.

Renault Group chairman Jean-Dominique Senard said upon announcing DeMeo’s departure:

...our company has returned to a healthy foundation, boasts an impressive range of products and has resumed growth.

DeMeo, 58, left for the role of CEO of Kering, a French luxury brand holding company that owns Gucci, Saint Laurent and Balenciaga.

See You

It may be said that DeMeo – who has no obvious successor – is getting out of Dodge while the getting is good. On top of European regulatory pressure to reduce emissions and Nissan’s weakness, Chinese automakers are rapidly increasing their share of a European market that once was the near-exclusive province of VW, Renault and other European manufacturers.

While some cooperation between Renault and Nissan remains, their mutual ownership ties are unwinding. This week, Nissan said it will be selling Renault shares to raise capital for its own new-model development. The automakers previously announced a reduction of their cross-holdings to 10% from 15%. The sale of 5% of Renault shares should generate about $640 million for Nissan.

Renault has been pursuing collaboration with Geely, the Chinese automaker and owner of Volvo that is tenth globally by unit sales and has been expanding into non-Chinese markets, especially in Europe. On Friday, Renault and Geely announced a joint venture – 73.6% owned by Renault – that would facilitate expansion of vehicle production and sales in Brazil, Renault’s fifth largest overseas market. The two automakers also own a factory jointly in South Korea.

If the relationship with Geely grows and prospers, it could constitute a potential safety net for Renault – and Renault shareholders – should the company run into existential difficulties.

It’s worth noting that Wall Street ignores RNSDF, which with a market capitalization of less than $13 billion (and a major stake owned by the French government) presents very few prospects for improved financial performance and thus isn’t really worth the time and effort of a top automotive equity analyst. Likewise, Seeking Alpha’s Quant analytic system declines to give the stock a rating, which should give investors pause.

Seeking Alpha analysts collectively rate Renault a buy for a variety of reasons and rationales.

My SELL rating on the stock rests on two basic pillars. First, so many promising public companies post consistent or nearly consistent growth in revenue, earnings and dividends; anyone who cares about his or her money can and should invest in a company with a much better income statement than Renault’s – and there are myriad options and choices.

Rules Change

Secondly, the global automotive industry is in a broad state of flux, which poses a major risk to all automakers, even ones that post much healthier numbers than Renault’s. As mentioned, the rising tide of Chinese automakers, whose cost basis, technology and cheap government financing pose a big advantage over Western incumbents with well-known names, appears likely to hobble (or eventually absorb) smaller and weaker players.

Moreover, each of the three major global automotive markets has a slightly different approach to reducing emissions and promoting vehicle electrification, making decisions of where to sell, where to manufacture and how much to invest in new tech (as opposed to partnering) extremely risky. Renault and Nissan made an aggressive early bet on batteries with the zero-emission Leaf, which likely cost the alliance at least $10 billion of capital.

The main risk to my Sell rating is that Renault will find a leader with the first-class financial, operational and diplomatic skills to reverse the company’s financial slide and restore growth in revenue and income – someone unusual, perhaps, like Sergio Marchionne, Fiat Chrysler’s CEO prior to his death in 2018. Marchionne came from outside the auto industry, though he arguably understood and increased shareholder value better than any of his contemporaries.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Oracle: Maintaining Buy Rating On Strong OCI Momentum And Unique Position In Multi-Cloud
data.description:
A few months ago, I published the article The Cloud Underdog? Why Oracle's 3% Market Share Signals a Strong Upside. In this article, I explained why I believe Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) has the potential to expand its market share and increase its market cap. Since then, the stock has risen 20%, compared to the S&P 500's -1.89%.

So what do we have here? Double-digit growth in RPO, leading growth metrics by a significant margin over competitors, fair valuation compared to (in my opinion) the most relevant peer group, innovation with AI, bright guidance, and the Stargate project which, if realized, could boost profits even further. Based on all that, I maintain my Strong Buy rating.

Why Is Oracle So Special?

Oracle provides cloud products, including enterprise cloud applications (SaaS), Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI), and advanced database services. In my opinion, the interesting story lies in OCI (Oracle Cloud Infrastructure). I noted in my last article the low market share of Oracle in the cloud sector (3%) and the aggressive potential for growth, and I think we are seeing the beginnings of that expansion.

In the latest earnings report, Oracle reported a 56% year-over-year growth in cloud RPO, driven by surging demand for Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI). The company said the demand is exponential and exceeding supply, which explains the elevated CapEx levels - a topic we'll elaborate later.

Oracle was a database provider, before successfully pivoting to the cloud, and is now positioned as a multi-cloud company. So the most interesting product is OCI, with the vector database oriented toward LLMs and the ability for multi cloud. One of Oracle's biggest advantages today is that its database can run directly on other cloud platforms like AWS and Azure. Most competitors don't allow this - their systems are locked into their own. Because of this flexibility, Oracle can now win large deals even with customers who have already committed to other cloud providers.

Another key product is Oracle 23AI, the newest version of Oracle's database, built for the AI era. It's designed to work directly with LLMs like ChatGPT and Grok. That means businesses can use their internal data with AI tools without custom connections - it's built to work together.

Larry Ellison, Oracle's founder and CTO, said during the last earning call:

We are the key enabler for enterprises to use their own data and AI models. No one else is doing that.

Oracle 23AI is also a vector database, which means the data is stored in a way that's easy for AI models to search and understand. According to CEO Safra Catz, demand for Oracle's AI-ready database is so high that they can't keep up. The company is still pushing customer contracts into the future because they don't yet have enough capacity. That's one reason Oracle's CapEx is growing fast - to build more data centers and meet that demand. And it is only the beginning, as the CEO Safra Ada Catz stated:

So the Oracle database is on fire. And it is only the beginning

As a result, Oracle expects cloud infrastructure revenue to grow over 70% this year, with cloud database services becoming one of the top three growth engines for the company.

Valuation

As I said in my last article, Oracle was expected to deliver double-digit growth, and it met the forecast with $15.9B in revenue, representing 11% YoY growth. Revenue growth increased from 3% in Q4 last year to 11% YoY, and based on the forecasts this growth trend is expected to continue between 11% to 13% in constant currency, or 12% to 14% in U.S. dollars. In Q4, operating cash flow was $6.2 billion, but free cash flow was negative at $2.9 billion because of high CapEx.

CapEx rose from $5.9B in Q3 to $9.1B in the last quarter, while operating cash flow grew from $5.9B in Q3 to $6.2 billion. There is no doubt we need to monitor the high CapEx closely in the next quarter, and we'll dive into it further next.

In terms of valuation metrics, forward EV/S stands at 10.15, compared to peers at 2.9. Forward P/E is 30.31x, compared to peers at 22.71x, and forward price to cash flow is 23.30, compared to peers at 18.74x.

I think it's important to highlight the management's commitment to shareholders, as the CEO said during the earning call:

We are committed to returning value to our shareholders through technical innovation, strategic acquisitions, stock repurchases, prudent use of debt and a dividend. This quarter, we repurchased a little over 1 million shares for a total of $150 million.

All these numbers imply a high valuation for a company with a half-trillion-dollar market cap. But if we dig deeper and compare Oracle to its most relevant peers in the cloud, we see a different picture.

Oracle Vs. Cloud Peers

Oracle's main growth engine is its cloud business. Therefore, it is appropriate to compare it to the major players in the cloud. When we compare it to the main players like Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) - AWS, and Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) - Azure, the numbers speak for themselves.

Oracle delivered an impressive 27% year-over-year growth in cloud, and 52% YoY growth in IaaS, more than AWS. Even more impressively, Oracle expects this momentum to continue, with a forecast of 70% cloud growth in FY26.

On the valuation side, Oracle actually looks a bit cheaper than its peers. While all three companies trade at high multiples, Oracle has the lowest forward P/E at 30.81, compared to over 34x for AWS and nearly 36x for Azure. It also offers a higher dividend yield than Microsoft and is much more cash-efficient than Amazon, based on price to cash flow.

It's also important to consider that Amazon and Microsoft operate in many sectors beyond cloud, and this gives them an advantage over Oracle. But if you're looking for pure exposure to the cloud sector, I believe that with the best valuation and the highest growth rate, the choice is clear.

The Bonus: Stargate Project

Stargate is a new company planning to invest $500 billion over the next few years into building AI infrastructure for OpenAI in the U.S. Oracle is set to be a key player in all of this.

As part of this effort, Oracle is reportedly planning to spend around $40 billion on advanced Nvidia (NVDA) chips to help build a large-scale data center for OpenAI in Abilene, Texas. This facility, scheduled to go live in mid-2026, will give OpenAI huge computing power and reduce its reliance on Microsoft Azure.

For Oracle, this is a major opportunity to grow its cloud business and close the gap with cloud peers like AWS, Azure, and Google. It's also important to note that Oracle's contracts for Stargate haven't been finalized yet, which means this project isn't even included in the company's current backlog numbers. Think about it... Oracle's outlook is already impressive without the Stargate project. So imagine what it could become if this deal goes through.

Risks: Capex And Debt

So like everything in life, there is another side and here it is: Oracle's CapEx. Oracle CapEx stands at $9.1B, which is 57% of revenue. Just for comparison, AMZN CapEx is $24.3B, 15.6% of revenue, and MSFT is $21.4B, which is 30% of revenue.

Therefore, Oracle's expenses are unusually high relative to revenue. It is important to consider that the increase in CapEx is due to the huge demand that Oracle is experiencing. It is still a risk that should be considered. The CEO said:

we are making large engineering investments to speed up the networking, the reliability of the network and lower the cost of the networking. So we're doing a bunch of things to lower our CapEx costs. But even if we do that, CapEx is going to go up because the demand right now seems almost insatiable. I mean I don't know how to describe it. I've never seen anything remotely like this. I mean people are calling up and asking us, please, can you find us more capacity.

In addition, Oracle currently holds approximately $85B in long-term debt. In a high interest rate environment, it is definitely a risk to consider. Investors should watch free cash flow and debt level closely in FY26 and FY27 to ensure the aggressive CapEx plan translates to profitable expansion.

Summary

Oracle has become one of the fastest growing players in the cloud and AI infrastructure sector. With strong momentum in OCI, unique positioning in multi-cloud, and innovative AI databases and products for the cloud. While the high CapEx and debt levels raise some concerns, they also reflect strong demand for its products. Compared to peers like Amazon and Microsoft, Oracle delivers faster cloud growth at a cheaper valuation. For investors looking for exposure to the AI-driven cloud sector, in my opinion, Oracle is the best choice. I maintain my Strong Buy rating.

data.title: The Rise Of 'Knightian Uncertainty' In Markets: When Risk Can't Even Be Measured
data.description:
What happens when the future is so unpredictable that traditional financial models no longer work? Jeff Evans, Vice President & Director, Lead of Empirical Research and PM Support at TD Asset Management, says markets have entered a period known as ‘Knightian uncertainty’—when risk can no longer be accurately measured. He discusses the implications for investors with MoneyTalk’s Kim Parlee.

Transcript

Kim Parlee - When markets face uncertainty, investors often turn to forecasts, historical trends, and data models for some sort of guidance. But what happens when the future is so unpredictable that those models don't really work? My next guest says we're now in a moment called Knightian uncertainty, where risk can't even be measured.

Jeff Evans is Vice President and Director and Lead of Empirical Research and PM Support at TD Asset Management. He joins me now. I have to tell you, my favorite thing in the world is learning new things. So it's been a real pleasure talking to you before and learning this. Welcome.

Jeff Evans - Excellent. Thank you very much for having me on.

Kim Parlee - It is a pleasure. What is Knightian uncertainty?

Jeff Evans - So this is a concept that goes back to the 1930s. So it probably gives a little bit of context. It was an uncertain period back then, and that sets the tone for how this was created and what a lot of these economists were thinking about. So it was economist Frank Knight who actually came up with this.

And I think the best way to describe it is actually to-- let's start with something a little bit more familiar, which would just be risk. So I think most of the people tuning into the show probably have a car, probably have a house or an apartment. And when you have those things, you probably have insurance. Always important because you just never know if sometimes something's going to go wrong.

Your car might be stolen. You might have a leaky faucet that causes water damage. For any one individual person, you may not know if it's going to happen or not. Whether your particular car is stolen, that's sort of a random roll of the dice. You never know. But from the insurance company's perspective, when you have millions of people, now you can start to calculate the probabilities.

Kim Parlee - These are what actuaries do all day, day in and day out, right?

Jeff Evans - Exactly. And you can start to get a sense of, in a typical year, how likely is it for somebody to have their car stolen, how likely is it for your house to be damaged, and then what's the cost to fix it. And if you have those two things, you put them together, that's what we would call risk. We don't know what the outcome is for any particular person, but we can basically create an insurance contract.

We can price it. Somebody is willing to take the other side of it for you. Knightian uncertainty is a completely different environment where we can't even calculate the probabilities. We just don't even know how likely it is that your car is going to be stolen or how likely it is to have something wrong with your house.

And so that's the environment that we're in today, is where you think about some of the things that are happening in the macroeconomy right now, lots of uncertainty around tariffs and changes in policy, lots of uncertainty around sustainability of US debt and the dollar, other geopolitical concerns. There's a lot of different moving parts. Sometimes, we can find data on that.

Sometimes, we can't. And really, the last time that this has happened, particularly on the tariffs, was over 100 years ago, in the 1930s, when the economy was very different than it is today. So that's really the key issue, is risk, you can calculate the probabilities. This is completely different, where we can't even go back and look at past data to try and get even a rough sense of how likely different scenarios or outcomes may be.

Kim Parlee - Which, I mean, contributes, that that is why we see the volatility we see right now, is because the markets are constantly re-evaluating, I assume, reacting in real time, right?

Jeff Evans - Exactly. You're always trying to recalibrate the probabilities. And when you look at what happened in April, for a lot of investors trying to say, well, What's the likely outcome? if the outcome is, I have no idea, are you willing to step in and buy assets at that point in time? And that's where you start to see these really aggressive 10%, 20% moves in a short span of time. It's because nobody can actually figure out what the base case is.

Kim Parlee - But we have had tariffs. We haven't had tariffs for a very long time, in fairness, but we have had some tariffs. So how do you think about that?

Jeff Evans - And the fact that we haven't had, really, a recent history with tariffs at all?

Kim Parlee - Yeah.

Jeff Evans - So the first challenge is exactly that. We haven't had them. We had a little preview. I, guess you could say, in 2018, but on a much smaller scale, on a very small subset of goods and really targeted on China, was the main focus. This is very different. It's much broad based or far more broad based than it was in 2018. We don't know how the global economy is going to react because there's so many more moving parts in that scenario.

Kim Parlee - Can you take us through your thinking, though? I mean, if you're a-- because I know you have some thoughts about, how do you think about this as a US consumer? Just maybe you can take us through it.

Jeff Evans - Well, so let's start with, what is the tariff? So that's challenge number one. If you're a US consumer and you don't know what the tariff is going to be today or six months from now, problem number one is, do you make a consumption choice today? Do you buy your car now if you need to, or is this going to be gone in a year and maybe I'll just delay a little bit?

So that's the first challenge. If you're a consumer or a business, figuring out if these are permanent or temporary tariffs is a major challenge. And the response that you have is going to be very different under those two scenarios. Particularly for companies, if it's a temporary tariff, maybe you just absorb this in margins.

You help your consumers out, you make sure that there's continued demand, and the tariff is gone in a year. So it's just a little bit of a hit for a short period of time, but your business stays intact. If this is a permanent tax, and it's increasingly seeming like that's the direction that this is going in, the responses will be very different. Why take the hit now?

Or why not just pass it on to a consumer, rip the Band-Aid off, and move on? We just don't know how businesses are going to react to this, and we're hearing very different things from management teams. Some are very concerned about the political pushback if they try to pass this through. Others recognize it as an existential threat.

If they don't pass this on, they go to business. So there's just a huge range of responses on how businesses respond to this. And then the consumer is a completely different piece because we don't know how much of this consumption on imports is discretionary versus nondiscretionary.

Maybe that 10th pair of shoes you really, really need, or maybe we can actually get by without them. There's estimates. And economists have been trying to put some numbers on this as best they can. But when you have a massive shock on this scale, you start to get into nonlinearities and scenarios where there is true uncertainty.

We don't know how the business reacts, how the consumer reacts. And then you put all these things together, trying to sketch an outcome is very, very difficult to do.

But people still have to estimate, right? And I think people still have to forecast. And so when you see the World Bank and the IMF and other major institutions-- they're still putting out forecasts. So how do we interpret that? How do they do it?

Kim Parlee - Exactly.

Jeff Evans - And they all look tired. Because I've talked to a lot of them. I feel like it's harder than it used to be.

It's been a much tougher year than previous years. So I think the first challenge in an environment like this, just winging it is probably not the right way to do it. Businesses do need some degree of planning at the end of the day. And if you think about a major CEO trying to make investment decisions, staffing decisions--

Kim Parlee - Which is why we've seen things stop, quite frankly. But yes.

Jeff Evans - Well, exactly. Because there's no certainty on the way things are going to plan out or play out. First of all, if you look at what some of these macro models are doing and the output, the forecasts themselves haven't necessarily changed that much. People are still saying, maybe the economy is going to grow 2% to 3%. That average growth is stable.

It's the distribution of outcomes that's widening out significantly. And if you take one example, if you look at the Fed in their dot plots back in March, the range of outcomes for US GDP growth was anything between negative 1 and plus 5. So if you just think of what that means for a business, you could be in recession with no demand for your product, or you could be in a huge expansion with an enormous amount of demand, and how do you plan?

Kim Parlee - And no supply. Yeah, yeah, yeah.

Jeff Evans - Exactly. So it's kind of a fascinating outcome. And if you step back and think about what a lot of these other institutions are doing, everybody still needs to forecast. But the key now is understanding how those forecasts are made. What are the models? What are the assumptions?

What are the range of outcomes? And ideally, as people who need to consume these forecasts and try to get some sensibility out of them, we want to try to find forecasts that are using different approaches, different methodologies. If they agree, great. That probably gives us more confidence that that's the direction things are going in.

And then if there's disagreements, that can also help inform, maybe, particular scenarios we should pay more attention to. The key is, really, don't take anything at face value. The forecasts are probably wrong. What we're really trying to do is even just get the direction right. That's really what people are grappling with, is, is it a recession or not?

Kim Parlee - I see my producer has spoken with you and has some notes about AI being probably more useful in this because it can just generate so many different outcomes more quickly to understand.

Jeff Evans - Exactly. And I guess the first thing is it's also very easy for me to sit here and say, it's really important to understand how these complex economic models work. The reality is, you'd have to get multiple degrees, a PhD, spend, probably, 10 years working at the World Bank to really understand how the models work. AI is great.

You can toss in some of the technical manuals on these models. And you're never going to fully understand it the way an expert will. But at a high level, you can start to understand the basic assumptions, the strengths, the weaknesses of different models. AI is great for that kind of high-level summary work.

And then particularly when you start comparing some of these models, it's not perfect, but it's a really good way if you're trying to-- if there's two or three forecasts, you want to understand how they make them, how they get there, AI is great at that. And then, as you said, it's also really good at scenario analysis.

We can use the hallucinations that these models have, ask AI to come up with five or six different scenarios, How would tariffs potentially ripple through the economy?, and then we start iterating. We'll pick a particular scenario, dig in a little deeper, test the quality of the assumptions. And so I think it'd be a really interesting tool. It's almost perfect made for this type of scenario, where we don't know the probabilities, but we can actually look at, maybe, broad-based-more scenario analysis.

Kim Parlee - We're talking about Knightian uncertainty and all the things that are happening. Let's talk about Section 899. And from the lens of, I'll say, American exceptionalism, how does this play into all this from your perspective?

Jeff Evans - So this is an interesting aspect of the Big Beautiful Bill that was added in that would basically be a tax on dividends that are distributed to foreign holders. So anybody who owns shares in US companies could potentially see the withholding tax rise from, in certain cases, as little as 0% to 5% all the way up to 50% over a period of time.

Kim Parlee - Which affects Canadians, as well, as being foreign. But anyway, yes.

Jeff Evans - Exactly. And then on top of that, it would be different curve rates, as well, for pension funds that have also, in some cases, have no tax. So lots of potential changes for people who have money invested in the United States. There's a few interesting reasons why this has shown up in the bill.

One is, if they word it properly, under the way the Congressional Budget Office looks at the bill and the funding, they get to count the revenues. And so there's lots of other spending things happening in the bill. This would be one area where they get to say, we have revenues coming in. And so that's quite important from an optics perspective to help get this bill passed.

The flip side of that is, though, it's very important that the wording does not require them to actually enforce this. What the administration is really trying to do is preserve optionality and selectively enforce this, if they want to, at certain points in time. So the goal, hopefully, is to have this as a negotiating tool. And then, really, as you go out over the next two or three years, if the tariff revenues don't come in in line with what the administration was expecting, this may be a potential fallback to help backstop revenues and pay for the bill.

But I think that's the way we're thinking about it. It was very important just to help get this bill passed from a funding perspective. And at least the way we're thinking about it, it's more of a long-term concern if tariffs don't work out as expected.

The tariffs and the funding doesn't work out as expected, the debt becomes larger and we start thinking about the reserve currency status and all that good stuff. How do you even think about the US if it doesn't become the default destination for capital going forward?

I think the biggest challenge, first of all, it really comes back to there's so many different moving parts at the moment. The bill on its own is potentially quite concerning. This could be something that blows out the budget deficit quite significantly. What we don't know is what level of tariffs are going to come in.

So that will be an offset. And then you could actually get some economic growth, which offsets as well. We've seen scenarios where you could actually get pretty close to a balanced budget. So there's a wide range of outcomes. It could be an 8% deficit. It could be almost a balanced budget.

It's pretty wild. And so we need to find that out. And I think, really, the only way we're going to do that is to let time go by and see. That uncertainty is a problem. When you have a major destination for capital like the United States, that has been founded on rules of law, very sustainable institutions, slow change, we've had a pretty significant shock.

And if we can have the shock here, we can have it elsewhere. What we ultimately come back to, though, is when investors start looking for alternatives. What we've seen in our research is that the biggest drivers of stock returns over time are profitability and earnings growth. Ultimately, what investors want is for earnings to go up over time because that tends to drive stock prices.

When we look around the world and try to find other locations that can mirror the US, it's very few, far, and in between. The vast majority of high-profitability companies with strong earnings growth are in the US. And the few companies that are outside of the US, they're profitable because they're selling into the US. There's just a very small pool of alternatives.

And the big question is, do we start to see some of the follow-on effects from this of other countries starting to invest? Do we see other different government programs globally to help drive earnings growth? We're not there today, though, and that's, I think, the core challenge, is there's very few opportunities that have the liquidity and the growth that the US offers.

Kim Parlee - OK. So given all this, then, and you've given us a lot-- I've got about 90 seconds. But in this world of Knightian uncertainty, then, you're saying the state still looks pretty compelling compared to everywhere else. But what do investors do?

Jeff Evans - So I think the first is recognize that this is uncertain. And when things are truly uncertain, diversification becomes incredibly important. Spread your investments around. Don't put everything into-- large-cap market-weighted indices are heavily skewed to the US.

It may have performed very well historically. Don't rely on that as the assumption going forward. Really revisit the portfolio and make sure that it's robust to different scenarios. We want lots of assets that will perform well in strong inflation. We want assets that perform well in weak inflation.

Doing that stress testing and making sure you have a diversified portfolio is the best way to navigate uncertainty. And then the next most important thing is to be cognizant of what you're paying for these assets. When you're paying 30 times earnings, a lot has to go well just to get a 10% return.

It's incredibly important in this environment to be aware of valuation. And again, just be cognizant that things may not play out the way the market expects. Having that little extra buffer can make a huge difference.

Kim Parlee - This is probably an unfair question to ask you with 10 seconds to go, but how long do you expect this uncertainty to last? Are we in a point in time, or is this the beginning of a longer time of uncertainty?

Jeff Evans - I think it will be a point in time. We will get to a point where tariffs are known, the rates are a little more predictable and stable. We're starting to see the output of tariffs and the impact on economic growth. I think we're going to have a much better handle on this in one to two years. It'll be bumpy, but I don't think this is going to last.
